title: Endometriosis
oneLiner: Chronic oestrogen-dependent condition where ectopic endometrial tissue causes pelvic pain, subfertility, and dysmenorrhea; affects ~10% of reproductive-age women.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ectopic pregnancy** – pregnancy test (β-hCG) + TVUS: any gestation sac outside uterus + pain + shock = surgical emergency.
- **Ovarian torsion** – sudden, severe unilateral pain + vomit + tender adnexal mass; TVUS with absent/abnormal flow = emergency laparoscopy.
- **Ruptured endometrioma** – rare but acute peritonitis + haemodynamic instability; massive free fluid on bedside US + rigid abdomen = immediate gynaecology + theatre.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Cyclical chronic pelvic pain (worse peri-menstrually)
  - Dysmenorrhoea (often severe, may start pre-menses)
  - Deep dyspareunia
  - Subfertility (30–50% of affected women)
  - Dyschezia / dysuria (cyclical, with or without bleeding)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Bimanual: tender uterosacral ligaments, fixed retroverted uterus, adnexal tenderness or mass
  - Speculum: may see blue or black ‘powder-burn’ nodules on cervix/posterior fornix
  - Normal exam does **not** exclude endometriosis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely** → **Less likely** with differentiators:
  - **Ectopic pregnancy** – always positive β-hCG; TVUS shows empty uterus + adnexal mass
  - **Ovarian torsion** – acute onset, unilateral, vomiting; US Doppler ↓ flow
  - **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)** – fever, cervical motion tenderness, discharge; elevated CRP/ESR, swabs positive
  - **Irritable bowel syndrome** – pain with bowel habit changes, no cyclical pattern; normal pelvic exam
  - **Interstitial cystitis** – bladder pain worse with filling, relieved by voiding; no cyclical component
  - **Adenomyosis** – bulky, tender uterus; cyclical heavy bleeding; but uterus symmetrical, no nodules
  - **Ovarian cyst** – simple cyst on US; pain not necessarily cyclical
- **Splitter:** Positive pregnancy test → ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise; no fever + cyclical pattern → endometriosis >> PID
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine β-hCG (pregnancy test) – **mandatory** in all reproductive-age women with pelvic pain
  - Urine dipstick – rule out infection
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + CRP – check for infection, anaemia
  - β-hCG (if urine positive)
  - CA125 – **not recommended** for diagnosis (raised in many benign/malignant conditions); only use if suspect ovarian malignancy
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) – endometriomas (hypoechoic cysts with ground-glass echogenicity), deep infiltrating nodules, fixed ovaries
  - Second-line: Pelvic MRI – better for deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) and bladder/bowel involvement
  - **Never use CT for diagnosis** – low sensitivity; only for complications (e.g., ureteric obstruction)
- **Special tests:**
  - Laparoscopy + histology – gold standard for definitive diagnosis (visible implants, fibrosis; biopsy shows endometrioid glands and stroma)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE (2024): Diagnosis can be made empirically based on symptoms and signs (especially cyclical pain + risk factors). Laparoscopy reserved when symptoms severe, treatment fails, or subfertility.
  - No single scoring system used acutely; for surgical staging: ASRM classification (but not needed on ward)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine β-hCG** – missing ectopic pregnancy causes preventable maternal death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Acute pain: NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS with food) + paracetamol 1g QDS; if severe → consider oral morphine 5–10mg [check local policy]
  - IV fluids if NBM / vomiting
  - Urgent gynaecology referral if:
    - Haemodynamic instability
    - Positive β-hCG with adnexal mass
    - Signs of peritonism / torsion
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue analgesia (regular NSAIDs + paracetamol; avoid opioids as first-line)
  - If admitted for pain control: check TVUS if not yet done
  - Start hormonal therapy if appropriate (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Liaise with gynaecology for further outpatient planning
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Conservative: regular simple analgesia + combined hormonal contraception (COC, patch, ring) – continuous dosing to avoid menstruation
  - Mirena™ (levonorgestrel-releasing IUS) – reduces dysmenorrhoea and bleeding; effective for 5 years
  - Progestogen-only (norethisterone, desogestrel, depot medroxyprogesterone)
  - GnRH analogues (goserelin / leuprorelin) – short-term (6 months) with add-back HRT if needed; for severe, refractory disease
  - Surgery: laparoscopic excision or ablation of endometriomas and implants; consider in subfertility or failed medical therapy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** NBM if awaiting surgery; otherwise crystalloid 1L/8h if oral intake poor
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if admitted overnight, BMI>40 adjust – [check local policy])
  - **Diet:** As tolerated; avoid constipation (encourage fibre + fluids)
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (if acute pain) or 12-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g daily) – with or after food
  - Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g daily)
  - Combined oral contraceptive pill (e.g., Microgynon™ 30 – 1 tablet daily continuously, i.e., no placebo break) – check no contraindications (migraine with aura, smokers >35, thrombophilia)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Naproxen 250–500mg PO BD – reduce dose if renal impairment
  - Desogestrel 75mcg PO daily (progestogen-only pill)
  - Mirena™ IUS – inserted by trained clinician, lasts 5 years
  - Norethisterone 5mg PO TDS (days 5–26 of cycle or continuously) – [check BNF for exact regimen]
  - Goserelin (Zoladex™) 3.6mg SC monthly for 6 months – [check local policy for add-back HRT]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in peptic ulcer, severe renal impairment, asthma (some patients); caution with anticoagulants
  - COCP: avoid in migraine with aura, smokers >35, hypertension, liver disease, history of VTE
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: reduce dose / avoid in eGFR <30; caution in cirrhosis
  - Paracetamol: safe in renal/hepatic impairment (max dose 2g/day if severe liver disease)
  - Progestogens/GnRH: no major dose adjustment needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **GnRH agonists: forgetting to prescribe add-back HRT for >6 months** → accelerated bone loss; always document duration and review plan
  - **COCP in undiagnosed migraine with aura** → increased stroke risk; ask about headache type before prescribing
  - **NSAIDs without renal function check** → acute kidney injury in dehydrated or older patient
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ruptured endometrioma (rare) → peritonitis, haemorrhage, need for emergency laparoscopy
  - Ovarian torsion (associated with endometriomas) → ovarian necrosis
  - Bowel obstruction (severe DIE with rectal wall involvement)
  - Ureteric obstruction → hydronephrosis, renal impairment
- **Chronic:**
  - Subfertility (tubal damage, adhesions, impaired ovarian reserve)
  - Chronic pelvic pain syndrome
  - Anaemia from heavy menstrual bleeding
  - Psychosocial impact (depression, relationship dysfunction)
sections.Prognosis: - Chronic relapsing condition; remission often achieved with menopause (natural or surgical). Not life-threatening but significantly impacts quality of life. Subfertility may require assisted reproduction. Recurrence after conservative surgery is common (~20–50% at 5 years).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a pregnancy test** in any reproductive-age woman with pelvic pain – even if she says “I can’t be pregnant”.
- **Consider Mirena™ early** for dysmenorrhoea + heavy bleeding – excellent adherence, low systemic hormones.
- **Refer to gynaecology if symptoms persist despite first-line NSAIDs + COCP** – don’t repeat cycles of simple analgesia without specialist input.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on CA125 to diagnose endometriosis** – false positives from ovarian cysts, fibroids, pregnancy, cancer; leads to unnecessary anxiety or imaging.
- **Do not prescribe opioids for chronic pelvic pain** – risk of dependence; treat the underlying endometriosis with hormones.
- **Do not dismiss cyclical pain as “period pain” in young women** – may delay diagnosis for years; always consider endometriosis if pain limits daily activities.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  1. Now or worsening severe pain not controlled by regular NSAIDs/paracetamol
  2. Fever, vomiting, or inability to pass urine/stool
  3. Heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking pad <1 hour) or signs of shock (feeling faint, rapid heart rate)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP within 2 weeks to review analgesia and hormonal therapy plan
  - Gynaecology clinic within 6–8 weeks if symptoms persist or new risk factors (subfertility, DIE)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / gynaecology reg urgently if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >20, HR >100, BP <90 systolic)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Unilateral, severe, constant abdominal pain with vomiting and peritonism (suspect torsion)
  - Acute hypotension + positive β-hCG (ectopic rupture – crash call)
  - Signs of bowel obstruction (distension, absolute constipation, vomiting)
  - Suspected ruptured endometrioma with haemodynamic instability (rare but need immediate theatre)

rawMarkdown:
## Endometriosis
**One-liner:** Chronic oestrogen-dependent condition where ectopic endometrial tissue causes pelvic pain, subfertility, and dysmenorrhea; affects ~10% of reproductive-age women.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ectopic pregnancy** – pregnancy test (β-hCG) + TVUS: any gestation sac outside uterus + pain + shock = surgical emergency.
- **Ovarian torsion** – sudden, severe unilateral pain + vomit + tender adnexal mass; TVUS with absent/abnormal flow = emergency laparoscopy.
- **Ruptured endometrioma** – rare but acute peritonitis + haemodynamic instability; massive free fluid on bedside US + rigid abdomen = immediate gynaecology + theatre.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Cyclical chronic pelvic pain (worse peri-menstrually)
  - Dysmenorrhoea (often severe, may start pre-menses)
  - Deep dyspareunia
  - Subfertility (30–50% of affected women)
  - Dyschezia / dysuria (cyclical, with or without bleeding)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Bimanual: tender uterosacral ligaments, fixed retroverted uterus, adnexal tenderness or mass
  - Speculum: may see blue or black ‘powder-burn’ nodules on cervix/posterior fornix
  - Normal exam does **not** exclude endometriosis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely** → **Less likely** with differentiators:
  - **Ectopic pregnancy** – always positive β-hCG; TVUS shows empty uterus + adnexal mass
  - **Ovarian torsion** – acute onset, unilateral, vomiting; US Doppler ↓ flow
  - **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)** – fever, cervical motion tenderness, discharge; elevated CRP/ESR, swabs positive
  - **Irritable bowel syndrome** – pain with bowel habit changes, no cyclical pattern; normal pelvic exam
  - **Interstitial cystitis** – bladder pain worse with filling, relieved by voiding; no cyclical component
  - **Adenomyosis** – bulky, tender uterus; cyclical heavy bleeding; but uterus symmetrical, no nodules
  - **Ovarian cyst** – simple cyst on US; pain not necessarily cyclical
- **Splitter:** Positive pregnancy test → ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise; no fever + cyclical pattern → endometriosis >> PID

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine β-hCG (pregnancy test) – **mandatory** in all reproductive-age women with pelvic pain
  - Urine dipstick – rule out infection
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + CRP – check for infection, anaemia
  - β-hCG (if urine positive)
  - CA125 – **not recommended** for diagnosis (raised in many benign/malignant conditions); only use if suspect ovarian malignancy
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) – endometriomas (hypoechoic cysts with ground-glass echogenicity), deep infiltrating nodules, fixed ovaries
  - Second-line: Pelvic MRI – better for deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) and bladder/bowel involvement
  - **Never use CT for diagnosis** – low sensitivity; only for complications (e.g., ureteric obstruction)
- **Special tests:**
  - Laparoscopy + histology – gold standard for definitive diagnosis (visible implants, fibrosis; biopsy shows endometrioid glands and stroma)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE (2024): Diagnosis can be made empirically based on symptoms and signs (especially cyclical pain + risk factors). Laparoscopy reserved when symptoms severe, treatment fails, or subfertility.
  - No single scoring system used acutely; for surgical staging: ASRM classification (but not needed on ward)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine β-hCG** – missing ectopic pregnancy causes preventable maternal death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Acute pain: NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS with food) + paracetamol 1g QDS; if severe → consider oral morphine 5–10mg [check local policy]
  - IV fluids if NBM / vomiting
  - Urgent gynaecology referral if:
    - Haemodynamic instability
    - Positive β-hCG with adnexal mass
    - Signs of peritonism / torsion
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue analgesia (regular NSAIDs + paracetamol; avoid opioids as first-line)
  - If admitted for pain control: check TVUS if not yet done
  - Start hormonal therapy if appropriate (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Liaise with gynaecology for further outpatient planning
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Conservative: regular simple analgesia + combined hormonal contraception (COC, patch, ring) – continuous dosing to avoid menstruation
  - Mirena™ (levonorgestrel-releasing IUS) – reduces dysmenorrhoea and bleeding; effective for 5 years
  - Progestogen-only (norethisterone, desogestrel, depot medroxyprogesterone)
  - GnRH analogues (goserelin / leuprorelin) – short-term (6 months) with add-back HRT if needed; for severe, refractory disease
  - Surgery: laparoscopic excision or ablation of endometriomas and implants; consider in subfertility or failed medical therapy
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** NBM if awaiting surgery; otherwise crystalloid 1L/8h if oral intake poor
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if admitted overnight, BMI>40 adjust – [check local policy])
  - **Diet:** As tolerated; avoid constipation (encourage fibre + fluids)
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (if acute pain) or 12-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g daily) – with or after food
  - Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g daily)
  - Combined oral contraceptive pill (e.g., Microgynon™ 30 – 1 tablet daily continuously, i.e., no placebo break) – check no contraindications (migraine with aura, smokers >35, thrombophilia)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Naproxen 250–500mg PO BD – reduce dose if renal impairment
  - Desogestrel 75mcg PO daily (progestogen-only pill)
  - Mirena™ IUS – inserted by trained clinician, lasts 5 years
  - Norethisterone 5mg PO TDS (days 5–26 of cycle or continuously) – [check BNF for exact regimen]
  - Goserelin (Zoladex™) 3.6mg SC monthly for 6 months – [check local policy for add-back HRT]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in peptic ulcer, severe renal impairment, asthma (some patients); caution with anticoagulants
  - COCP: avoid in migraine with aura, smokers >35, hypertension, liver disease, history of VTE
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: reduce dose / avoid in eGFR <30; caution in cirrhosis
  - Paracetamol: safe in renal/hepatic impairment (max dose 2g/day if severe liver disease)
  - Progestogens/GnRH: no major dose adjustment needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **GnRH agonists: forgetting to prescribe add-back HRT for >6 months** → accelerated bone loss; always document duration and review plan
  - **COCP in undiagnosed migraine with aura** → increased stroke risk; ask about headache type before prescribing
  - **NSAIDs without renal function check** → acute kidney injury in dehydrated or older patient

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ruptured endometrioma (rare) → peritonitis, haemorrhage, need for emergency laparoscopy
  - Ovarian torsion (associated with endometriomas) → ovarian necrosis
  - Bowel obstruction (severe DIE with rectal wall involvement)
  - Ureteric obstruction → hydronephrosis, renal impairment
- **Chronic:**
  - Subfertility (tubal damage, adhesions, impaired ovarian reserve)
  - Chronic pelvic pain syndrome
  - Anaemia from heavy menstrual bleeding
  - Psychosocial impact (depression, relationship dysfunction)

### Prognosis
- Chronic relapsing condition; remission often achieved with menopause (natural or surgical). Not life-threatening but significantly impacts quality of life. Subfertility may require assisted reproduction. Recurrence after conservative surgery is common (~20–50% at 5 years).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a pregnancy test** in any reproductive-age woman with pelvic pain – even if she says “I can’t be pregnant”.
- **Consider Mirena™ early** for dysmenorrhoea + heavy bleeding – excellent adherence, low systemic hormones.
- **Refer to gynaecology if symptoms persist despite first-line NSAIDs + COCP** – don’t repeat cycles of simple analgesia without specialist input.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on CA125 to diagnose endometriosis** – false positives from ovarian cysts, fibroids, pregnancy, cancer; leads to unnecessary anxiety or imaging.
- **Do not prescribe opioids for chronic pelvic pain** – risk of dependence; treat the underlying endometriosis with hormones.
- **Do not dismiss cyclical pain as “period pain” in young women** – may delay diagnosis for years; always consider endometriosis if pain limits daily activities.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  1. Now or worsening severe pain not controlled by regular NSAIDs/paracetamol
  2. Fever, vomiting, or inability to pass urine/stool
  3. Heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking pad <1 hour) or signs of shock (feeling faint, rapid heart rate)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP within 2 weeks to review analgesia and hormonal therapy plan
  - Gynaecology clinic within 6–8 weeks if symptoms persist or new risk factors (subfertility, DIE)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / gynaecology reg urgently if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >20, HR >100, BP <90 systolic)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Unilateral, severe, constant abdominal pain with vomiting and peritonism (suspect torsion)
  - Acute hypotension + positive β-hCG (ectopic rupture – crash call)
  - Signs of bowel obstruction (distension, absolute constipation, vomiting)
  - Suspected ruptured endometrioma with haemodynamic instability (rare but need immediate theatre)
